Avalanche Summary: Sustained sun and warming intitiated these slides.
Sustained sun and warming intitiated these slides.

Small wet loose avalanche(s) triggered over steep rollovers on small test slopes while skiing back to the loop. These avalanches failed predictably on the old snow/new snow interface and moved slowly downhill entraining storm snow until the slope flattened out (picture attached).
This slide occurred on a new glide crack that recently opened up above a larger crack first observed in late Feb.
Triggered when rider jumped onto slope and fell. Rider able to esape debris.
Estimate on date and size, did not get clear view of runouts. Largest continuous crown estimated over 2000' wide on maps in office.
Ski cut 38* test slope and triggered a predictable soft wind slab avalanche.

After observing crown of intentionally triggered wind slab listed above, while skiing off of the bed surface of the wind slab avalanche a snowboarder triggered a deeper persistant slab avalanche. The snowboarder was caught and carried ~75ft downhill. The snowboarder came to rest on top of the debris. The avalanche failed on a layer of recently buried near surface facets under recently consolidated surface snow.

This avalanche was unintentionally triggered at the flank, from a connected lower angle slope while descending. The slab propagated up (well above the rider) and across the small slope. Rider was easily able to avoid getting caught.
Triggered this while walking a 33 degree ridgeline. This slab wrapped from an east facing aspect all the way around to a west facing aspect. Moving debris pushed me downhill approximately 15 feet.
Released below rocks on leeward slope about 250 ft below ridge. Crown linked across small terrain features.
Small, narrow natural avalanche that broke below rocks on leeward slope. Debris did not trigger slab on slope below.
Saw a few large debris piles that looked like they failed overnight or in the early hours of the morning.

location: Within The Bubble marked on Map Location above
Avalanche Summary: I'd say about 6 inches of new snow slid freely on the smooth icy crust.  The crusts in this area were patchy but were found to be quite hard and supportable a week ago.
Cornice fall triggered a Hard Slab 2 feet thick. The slope did not have a lot of room for it to propagate widely. Rocky terrain and thin snowpack reduced the likelihood for that.
Various old piles of wet debris. Unknown date.
Suspect the wet loose observed on the high point above marion failed on the first day of solar after a few inches of snow fell.
I'm guessing it was a hard slab. Failed just below ridgeline. Crown looked crisper than old crowns observed on Cameahwait.
Avalanche failed on adjacent slope after intentionally triggering a small avalanche.
A wet loose ran down and touched the marion lake trail. Suspect this failed during rain event?

Large avalanche crown observed (photo attached as well) from Snowslip's E ridge above Shed 7. Estimated date of occurrence given the fresh snow obscuring the debris. The avalanche may have been triggered by a natural smaller slab avalanche above, but it was hard telling from our vantage point..
Details on elevation and crown dimensions are estimated. Looks to have failed on an old persistent weak layer.
Debris piled on road. Originated in steep rocky terrain. Could not see start zone.
Debris piles on road. Originated in steep rocky terrain. Could not see start zone.
Initiated in surface wet snow but then gouged down deeper into the snowpack
Several loose dry in steep rocky terrain.  The longest (not pictured) ran ~400 ft.
A couple loose wet in the sunniest, rockiest terrain.
Original avalanche observation is posted on 3/8/23.

Appears to be a cornice fall onto the slope. The crown at the top of the slide seems to be around 5 ft. Did not notice crowns and debris from below, in Rescue Creek. Noticed when dropped into run. Party reports it's possible that they triggered the cornice fall remotely  from opposite side of ridge while ascending.
Small soft slab of new/ drifted snow released while cutting across a short, steep slope.

Best guess for timing is similar to large natural avalanche on Nyak (March 2, 2023) with possible additional more recent avalanche since then as some of the debris appears to be more fresh (less buried in recent snowfall).
Suspect this failed on on the 2nd when winds increased the day after Noisy basin picked up an inche of water.
Best guess for timing is similar to large natural avalanche on Nyak (March 2, 2023) with partially refilled crown and debris covered in recent snowfall.
Best guess for timing is similar to large natural avalanche on Nyak (March 2, 2023) with partially refilled crown and no view of debris/runout.
Turns on a short, very steep slope produced a wet loose sluff that quickly entrained the moist surface snow. Had the pitch been continuous, it easily could have grown into a D2 or larger.
Ski cut produced a small soft slab on the lee side of a mid-elevation ridge.
Could not see debris field but  guessing HS rather than SS
Fracture line partially visible midway up west bowl on Whitefish Mountain. Coudn't make out details/ dimensions because of poor visibility and recent and drifted snow.

2 slides across groomed trail (previously reported) that occurred before Sunday (based on convo w NMA). Recent and drifted snow on debris and crown obscured dimensions. Some of crowns still visible; debris ran to creek but not up other side. 6-10 ft debris on road. Uppermost 2 of 3 paths. Passable on sleds thanks to carved out trough. .
Reported by 2 observers 3/5; a 3rd observer reported seeing the crown on 2/25. Possibly triggered by cornice fall. Runout not visible.

location: Grant Ridge/ S. Fork Tunnel Ridge
Avalanche Summary: Crown face and bed surface partially filled in. Ran from ridgeline, then broke mmuch wider severl hundred feet down. Couldn't see runout or full extent of fracture line. Guessing hard slab on old snow.
I did not see the starting zone of either of these avalanches. Therefore I can not fill out all of the data accurately.

Another wind drifted test slope had propagation.

location: Cascadilla Creek Nyack Mtn NE face
Avalanche Summary: D2 to D3. Crowns running several hundred feet and ranging from 1-4+ feet thick.

location: SE-facing slopes off the Skiumah/Great Bear divide
Avalanche Summary: Several more crowns further up the basin and looker's right
Old crown. Appeared 5 feet thick.

location: Slopes to the west above Skiumah Lake
Avalanche Summary: Uncertain bed surface/failure layer and crown length
Crown details estimated. Thinking this might be a hard slab failing on old PWL
Observed from Java East along the rail. Could only view part of the crown, D2 at least
Observed from Java East along the rail. May be the same avalanche reported on Feb 20th? Could only view part of the crown. D2 at least.
Looked to be a big hard slab. Visibility limited by fog. Elevation and crown size estimated.
Rough estimate of the number of avalanches observed not already accounted for. Counted roughly 12 D2 and D3 size avalanches.
Observed three crowns. Appeared to be soft slabs. Varying aspects.
Width varied between 40-150 feet
I could not see the crowns and therefore can not estimate slab characteristics
We estimated that the wind loaded the slope to the tipping point. We could not safely access the crown.
Observed 5+ D2s or D3s off Mt. Penrose up Chrystal Drainage. Crowns spanned long distances and wrapped around terrain features.
Rough estimate of the number of avalanches observed not already accounted for.

This slab failed on facets just below the mid-January crust. We traced crownline 1200 feet and it conitnued out of site. Visibility was poor.
Knocked me off my feet. Carried a very short distance before coming to a stop on a small bench.
Muliple loose dry triggered intentionally on descent. Easy to manage. Few cracked and behaved like soft slab.
Triggered a small soft wind slab avalanche on a north/northeast aspect off running rabbit while traversing a ridgeline. It broke 25-30ft across and ran ~200 vertical feet.
photo taken from 48.18783, -113.94605- standing at 6465 feet. Most of the details unknown.
Difficult to see debris. crown evident and observed in "pocket" terrain.

It ran sometime either this morning (2/9) or last evening after 5pm, when I photographed the glide crack on the left and there was no sign whatsoever of the patch weakening that has now failed on the right.
Slabs failed at two interfaces. One is a new/old snow from last night on graupel. The second is on a layer of decomposing stellar dendrites that fell last week.
Fairly obscured view, but was able to see crown and debris looking towards the shoulder of grant. This slide was itty bitty relative to the terrain.
Saw debris from side of Blaine. Unknown type. Possible cornice fall or small wind slab.
Observed three wind slabs.
Caught and carried 2 riders over a cliff band.
Unknown type. Guessing soft slab. Blown in crown.
Small loose dry triggered on small rollovers at all elevations.
Found some lightly wind drifted features and freshly wind drifted snow was reactive with ski cuts.
Guess on size.
Recent wet loose debris in path 1163.  Could not see where these started.

location: Swan Range South of Swan Lake
Avalanche Summary: Small crowns that had drifted back in.  Could not make out track.  Did not approach to look for debris piles.

location: Swan Range South of Swan Lake
Avalanche Summary: Small crown on a convexity.
One slab was hard. This slab propagated and didn't slide far due to the terrain we were in.
Loose dry on west aspects.

location: Running Rabbit Mtn - S face Path 1163
Avalanche Summary: Only debris in the runout clearly visible. Likely a wind slab. Released in upper part of the path but visibility obscured by fog.

location: Running Rabbit Mtn - SE face Shed 10
Avalanche Summary: Only debris in the runout clearly visible. Likely a wind slab. Released in upper part of the path but visibility obscured by fog.
Small predictable storm slab avalanches triggered in steeper pockets of rolling terrain.
We saw a few chunky piles of debris that had wind blown snow on them.
Stomping at the top of very steep slopes produced small but long running sluffs in the new snow.
Small natural sluffs in most very steep (40*) terrain features.
Individual mentioned it did not appear to be a windslab. Unknown size.
Thin wind slab
Slabs consisted of wind-drifted snow.
Small, steep pocket on larger slope that cracked when snowbiker landed a jump.
Triggered several very small but long running sluffs on very steep slopes near the canyon floor.
Walked up to the ridgeline and gave one soft stomp and triggered the first slab. This slab failed between two naturals that must have ran a few days ago.
Slab failed at a convexity from a falling cornice that we triggered. Again, this slab also failed between two other naturals.
Looked like 6'+ debris and 150' wide.  Unsure of elevation of start zone.  Fresh from last warm period, distinct from other N aspect loose wet debris from Santa Soaker.
Recent wet slides observed at all aspects
Older debris pile.  Possible loose wet from mid-January warming.

Terminus at 48.51310, -114.31549 piled up around 120 cm depth, overflowed road and small amount of debris ran to lower road. Path as high as can see, assume full distance but did not appear to step down. Snow pit obs next to road here.
General summary of avalanches we observed from Friday's warmup. Saw roughly 10 wet loose avalanches on all aspects at middle elevation. Most were small/medium in size.
Vague details. Observed from a long ways away. Crown appeared fairly recent. Best guess of timing would be during recent warming and rain.

location: SE Steep chutes above Dickie Creek

Avalanche Summary: Terminus at 48.51177, -114.31392 deposited estimated 60 cm on upper Rd. Appeared to squiggle through woods. Did not step down to lower layers, ran on Xmas crust
Dry snow moving on rain crust. Remote triggered. No riders injured.
Deep Slab/Glide. Bed surface is a smooth polished slab of bedrock.
Failed within recent storm snow
On a steep sidewall of the gully in the bottom of the path
Photo was sent to me by a friend… I encouraged him to submit an observation w/ more details
Fracturing below moist snow 12/23
Another slide triggered with a cornice drop that didn't involve as much debris.
Triggered two soft slabs of drifted snow with cornice drops from the ridge. Car-sized cunks of cornice, though actual slab debris was more like D1.5.  Bed surface seemed to be older wind slab.

Observed from ridgeline on the other side of the drainage. Flat light/moderately poor visibility. Confidence poor on initiation/crown location/s, but significant debris filling the gully was apparent. Estimated size 3 given the path characteristics and other recent activity reported. Would need a better look for a more accurate report on this slide.
Appeared to be a Glide Avalanche. See Photos.
Observed from a distance. Upper face of Penrose appeared to have numerous crowns stretch most of the east face. Some looked to go down to the rock.

location: Path 1163 S face Running Rabbit Mtn
Avalanche Summary: Main gully on looker's left headwall. Gouged down to the ground in track and runout entraining all the snow (photo)

location: Just south of Blue Rock cut
Avalanche Summary: Stopped in highway ditch. Damaged some vegetation. Gouged to the ground.
The only crown observed today not associated with a wet loose (photo). Mostly obscured from view, details lacking

location: Blue Rock cut, between Crystal and Stanton Creeks on US Highway 2
Avalanche Summary: Debris from 4 of the slides blocked the eastbound lane of US Highway 2. Debris 3-4 ft deep and 20-30 ft wide.
Assume this slide ran later in the storm since wet debris overran smoother looking debris.
Information based on photos and description.

location: Road cuts adjacent to the North Fork road
Avalanche Summary: These were noted on steep road cuts and cutbanks adjacent to the North Fork river. Aspects included both east and west. Vertical fall varied between 20-80 feet.

location: Shed 7 West SE face Snowslip Mtn
Avalanche Summary: Looks to have run on the Dec 20 storm interface. (photo)

location: Path 1163 S face Running Rabbit Mtn
Avalanche Summary: Could not see uppermost starting zone and uncertain if this may have been a slab?

location: SE face Mount Shields above Shields Ck
Avalanche Summary: 2 separate avalanches D2-2.5. Could not see debris terminus (photo)

location: Shed 4D Cutbank E face of Snowslip Mtn
Avalanche Summary: Reported by work train crew. Debris stopped just short of the rail. Looks to have gouged to the ground. (photo)
Broke at ground. Entrained a quite the debris for such a little slope.
Observed a number of debris piles down into Dickey. All covered in recent snow.

3 large debris piles in main fork of Dickey Creek. Debris included trees up to 12" diameter, was 3m+ deep in places and showed evidence of one path repeating during the storm with a wet pathway through the original debris pile! Could not clearly see crowns but am banking on WS given amount of the debris and adjacent wet slab crowns seen.
Only could see the debris piles in the runouts, but they were quite large for not having obvious crown lines (hard to see at distance).

location: Road cuts adjacent to the North Fork road
Avalanche Summary: These were noted on steep road cuts and cutbanks adjacent to the North Fork river. Aspects included both east and west. Vertical fall varied between 40-80 feet.
Estimated slab depth and width from a distance, but saw definitive crownline and assume that it broke in the old persistent weak snow.
1 slide appeared to have descended a fairly long way down the valley in the gully.
Upper crown appeared to be a dry slab. Lower crown looked more like it failed as a wet slab given the jagged nature of the crown line.

location: Path 1163 Headwall S face Running Rabbit Mtn

Avalanche Summary: Some looked to have been snowmobile triggered. But at last two were naturals that occurred between 1800-1900 hrs. (photo)
Triggered by plowing debris from railroad.
Bed surface looked close to ground, with little snow or blowing snow obscuring crown.
Crown on ESE-facing burn just below ridgeline. Wrapped across several terrain features and through burned snags. Couldn't see runout of debris.Date, slab thickness and width estimates.

Higher, more recent-looking slide on skiers right at top, at typical entry to bowl. Thick crown but not wide. Similar sized slide ~300 ft lower and further skiers left in bowl. More debris on bed surface.
Crown mostly blown in. Extent of debris visible from highway.

Two wide crowns visible skiers below ridge extending south from Mt. Cameahwait. Not in main bowl, as is typical. Both seemed large and likely the result of wind loading on steep, unsuppported face, though southern crown wrapped around a nose/ sub ridge.

location: SE face of Running Rabbit Mtn - Railroad Shed 10 feeder path
Avalanche Summary: Runout was at least to 5400', likely further, but out of view. Likely this failed on Nov. facets given the slab thickness.

location: South face of Running Rabbit Mtn - Railroad Path 1163
Avalanche Summary: Crown was mostly drifted in. Dimensions are rough estimates. Debris ran to 4600 ft. Uncertain if this failed on Nov. facets.
Crown in sparse trees on northeast facing slope above lake.

location: East Face of Nyack Mountain, Cascadilla Creek
Avalanche Summary: At least 3 partially filled crowns on the east face of Nyack Mountain above Cascadilla Creek. Generally at base of steepest terrain. Highest about 7000 ft. Possibly triggered by sluffing. Couldn't tell extent of runout; whole apron rough with debris.

location: Essex High Rustler/ Backdoor Chute/ Gunbarrel
Avalanche Summary: Crown mostly blown in except skiers right side. Ran from top, just below trees. Couldn't see extent of runout.
On apron below headwall. Deep crown, possibly because slope filled with sluff debris.

location: South face of Snowslip Mtn - Railroad Shed 7 West
Avalanche Summary: Crown drifted back in, dimensions are rough estimates. Thinking this likely failed on Nov. facets given the crown and visible ground features.
several small soft slabs failing in the storm snow on steep cut banks above the Hwy. These were 3-5" thick and mostly only propagated 20-30" but a few pushing 50'.

location: No name peak N side Noisy Creek
Avalanche Summary: N R3 D1 at 5500' S above cliff supported slope.     N R1 D1 at 6200' S, small part of larger bowl.
See details above. Would have run further downslope if slope angle were steeper.
Results from avalanche mitigation with explosives.

Seen from distance; size and date guesstimated.
